title: Strengthening Mechanisms in Low Density Fe–26Mn–xAl–1C Steels
abstract_summary: This study investigates the effect of Al content (2.59, 5.84, 9.92, 11.58 wt%) on yield strength and strengthening mechanisms in Fe-26Mn-xAl-1C low-density steels. Microstructural characterization reveals fully austenitic structures at low Al, while ferrite and nanosized κ-carbides form at higher Al contents. Quantitative assessment distinguishes solid solution, grain boundary, precipitation, and ferrite strengthening contributions, showing precipitation hardening by κ-carbides is dominant, being 1.78 times the solid solution hardening effect.
research_objective: To elucidate and quantitatively assess the role of Al content on the variation of yield strength and the underlying strengthening mechanisms in low-density Fe-26Mn-xAl-1C steels.
main_process_variables: Hot rolling: heated at 1150 °C, initial rolling temperature 1050 °C, total reduction 85% to 3 mm thick strip
Heat treatment: solution treatment at 1100 °C for 1 h, water quenching
Tensile testing: room temperature, strain rate 10^-3 s^-1
Microhardness testing: FM-700 tester, 5 kg load
Microscopy: optical microscopy with etchant (1 ml HCl, 100 ml methanol, 4 g picric acid); TEM with twin-jet electropolishing in 30% nitric acid in methanol at -30 °C
Additional grain size variation in 6Al steel by different heat treatment schedules
expected_information_axis: microstructure (grain size, phase fractions, κ-carbide presence)
mechanism (solid solution, grain boundary, precipitation, ferrite strengthening)
figure_evidence (optical micrographs, TEM images, stress-strain curves, Hall-Petch plot, strengthening contribution diagram)

key_sections.purpose: Reviews low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels and identifies the need to clarify Al's effect on strengthening mechanisms.
Describes the preparation, processing, and testing of the four steels with varying Al content.
Presents microstructure evolution, mechanical properties, quantitative analysis of strengthening contributions, and discussion of κ-carbide formation.
Summarizes the roles of Al, especially the dominant precipitation strengthening effect.
